title: ‘The Deb’ Star Charlotte MacInnes Attacks Rebel Wilson In Appeal Against Court Defeat
full_text: Skip to main content By Jake Kanter Jake Kanter International Investigations Editor @Jake_Kanter More Stories By Jake Mark Jermin, TikTok-Famous Agent Who Discovered ‘Harry Potter’ & ‘Wednesday’ Stars, May Have Broken UK Agency Law Paramount’s Takeover Of Warner Bros. Discovery Gets EU Greenlight BBC Considers Locking Down iPlayer For Viewers Who Refuse To Pay License Fee View All July 24, 2026 3:56am Rebel Wilson with Charlotte MacInnes in 2023 Getty The chaotic legal fight over Rebel Wilson ‘s directorial debut is far from over. The Deb star Charlotte MacInnes has formally appealed after the Federal Court of Australia ruled that Wilson did not defame her in a series of social media posts from July 2024. MacInnes claimed that Wilson damaged her reputation by alleging that she was sexually harassed by The Deb producer Amanda Ghost, and then walked back her claims to benefit her career. Justice Elizabeth Raper, the Australian judge who presided over the case, did not agree, ruling this week that “MacInnes has failed to establish that the publications have caused or were likely to cause serious harm.” Watch on Deadline Related Stories Legal Rebel Wilson Triumphs In Explosive Court Battle With 'The Deb' Co-Star Charlotte MacInnes Trailers Rebel Wilson's 'The Deb' Gets First Trailer & Digital Release Date; Pic Sparked High-Profile Legal Battles MacInnes’ legal team has filed an eight-page appeal, in which they argue that Justice Raper “erred” in reaching her conclusions. The filing also goes on the attack against Wilson. MacInnes argued that the judge failed to give “adequate weight” to matters that “affected” Wilson’s reputation. The appeal accused Wilson of giving “false evidence” and “destroying, concealing or otherwise failing to produce relevant documents.” The appeal also accuses Justice Raper of failing to take proper account of Wilson’s role in a so-called “smear website,” which made incendiary allegations about Ghost being the “Indian Ghislaine Maxwell.” Wilson has denied any involvement in the website. Representatives for Wilson have been approached for comment. At the heart of the case is an incident in September 2023, when Ghost shared a bath with MacInnes in a Sydney apartment after they had been swimming on Bondi beach. Ghost and MacInnes do not deny that they bathed together , but both say it was a non-sexual encounter, during which they were wearing swimsuits, after Ghost experienced a medical incident. Wilson claimed that MacInnes told her that the incident made the young actress uncomfortable. Wilson further alleged that MacInnes later withdrew her complaint after being given career opportunities by Ghost. MacInnes denied that the incident made her uncomfortable, suing Wilson over four Instagram posts, in which the Bridesmaids star discussed the events. Justice Raper agreed with Wilson. “MacInnes changed her story about feeling uncomfortable about what happened in the bathroom,” the judge ruled, adding that Wilson’s social media posts about MacInnes were not defamatory “by reason of these findings.” Justice Raper was not convinced by the testimony of MacInnes, Ghost, and Wilson but ultimately deemed that Wilson’s actions after the bathing incident supported her story. “I have serious misgivings as to the truthfulness of the testimony of each of Ms. MacInnes, Ms. Ghost and Ms. Wilson,” the judge said. “Whilst I accept that Ms. Wilson has a propensity to exaggerate events and her evidence could be found to be untruthful on occasion, the evidence establishes that Ms. Wilson acted in a manner consistent with a complaint being made and her own contemporaneous documentary trail supports this.” As well as the proceedings in Australia, The Deb ‘s producers are suing Wilson in the Los Angeles Superior Court, accusing the Pitch Perfect star of pursuing an unjustified vendetta after being denied a writing credit on The Deb . title: Fred Tsui Named Executive Director of Hong Kong International Film Festival Society
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 3:43am PT Fred Tsui Named Executive Director of Hong Kong International Film Festival Society By Naman Ramachandran Plus Icon Naman Ramachandran Latest Indian Epic ‘Ramayana’ Lands at Sony Pictures for International Distribution 14 hours ago Venice Lesbian Drama ‘Big Little Things’ Boards Moebius Entertainment for Worldwide Sales 23 hours ago Venice Title ‘The Difficult Bride’ Taps Films Boutique for World Sales (EXCLUSIVE) 24 hours ago See All Hong Kong International Film Festival Society The Hong Kong International Film Festival Society has appointed Fred Tsui , founder and CEO of Moebius Entertainment , as its new executive director, the organization said Friday. Tsui succeeds Albert Lee and assumes the role Sept. 23, reporting to HKIFFS chair Dr. Wilfred Wong and the board. In the new post, Tsui will oversee the society’s full range of initiatives: its marquee event, the Hong Kong International Film Festival, along with the Hong Kong – Asia Film Financing Forum and its HAF Film Lab, the Summer International Film Festival and the HKIFF Cine Fan strand. Related Stories Justin Baldoni Speaks Out on Blake Lively Legal Battle: 'There Have Been So Many Painful Things That Have Been Spoken Into Existence' Tsui arrives with more than 30 years of experience touching nearly every corner of the business, including distribution, financing, broadcasting, exhibition and media operations alongside international sales and co-production. That range has connected him with filmmakers, financiers, distributors, exhibitors, sales agents and festival programmers across Asia, Europe and North America. Popular on Variety He currently runs Moebius Entertainment as founder and chief executive, having built the outfit into a respected international sales agency for celebrated Asian titles. Under his watch, the company has crafted global sales strategies, lined up distribution deals across major territories and steered festival campaigns for award-winning films. The appointment lands amid an active stretch for Moebius: the company recently boarded worldwide sales for Michelle Zhou’s debut feature “Big Little Things,” selected for the Horizons section of the Venice Film Festival, and picked up sales rights outside Taiwan to Fiona Roan Feng-i’s “Happily Ever After,” set to premiere in the Toronto Film Festival’s Special Presentations section. Before launching Moebius, Tsui spent more than two decades at Media Asia Film, eventually taking charge of the studio’s international sales and co-production division as general manager. In that post, he helped extend one of Hong Kong’s major studios onto the world stage, running its global sales operation and striking co-production deals that boosted Hong Kong cinema’s international profile. Earlier in his career, Tsui held senior roles at PCCW and Asia Television, gaining experience in organizational leadership, media operations, strategic thinking and content oversight. Outside the executive suite, Tsui is also known as a film critic and has sat on juries at festivals including those in Hong Kong, Edinburgh, Jecheon, Tallinn, Seattle, Udine, Sitges, Sonoma and Skip City in Japan. He also sits on the Hong Kong Film Development Council. He also belongs to the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences (AMPAS) and the Hong Kong Film Critics Society. In past years he sat on the boards of the Independent Film & Television Alliance (IFTA) in the U.S. and the MIA International Audiovisual Market in Rome. The Sitges – International Fantastic Film Festival of Catalonia has honored him with its Maria Honorifica Award. “We are delighted to welcome Fred to HKIFFS,” said Wong. “Fred combines strategic leadership with an exceptional understanding of the international film landscape. Throughout his career, he has built bridges between Hong Kong cinema and the global marketplace while earning the trust and respect of filmmakers, festival directors and industry leaders worldwide. Just as importantly, he approaches cinema from the perspectives of an executive, a critic, a festival juror and a lifelong cinephile. We believe he is uniquely qualified to lead HKIFFS into its next chapter, further strengthening its role as one of Asia’s most important cultural institutions and international film platforms.” HKIFFS operates as a nonprofit, non-governmental charity built around fostering film as art and culture, running the HAF financing forum in tandem with the festival. It launched a monthly Cine Fan repertory strand in 2013 and, in 2021, restructured its industry-facing services into HKIFF Industry, which backs non-mainstream production via co-productions, festival guidance and international distribution under the HKIFF Collection label. “It is a tremendous honor to join HKIFFS, an institution that has played such a pivotal role in the cultural life of Hong Kong and in promoting Asian cinema internationally,” said Tsui. “Having worked with film festivals throughout my career – as a sales executive, producer, juror, critic and audience member – I have seen first-hand the unique role HKIFF plays in connecting filmmakers, the industry and audiences. I look forward to working with the board, our partners and the HKIFFS team to build on the society’s remarkable legacy while broadening its international reach, nurturing new talent, deepening audience engagement and ensuring Hong Kong remains at the forefront of global film culture.” Amazon Signs Wide-Ranging Deal With Kevin Durant and Rich Kleiman’s Boardroom Spanning Podcasts, Live Events and More LISTEN: Mike Hopkins on Prime Video’s Evolution, the Bundling Boom, How MGM Changed Amazon, the Future of Bond and No Regrets on ‘Melania’ Prime Video Launches Games With Luna App Integration; ‘Tomb Raider,’ James Bond Titles Won’t Be in the Mix Luca Guadagnino’s Nearly Finished Sam Altman Movie ‘Artificial’ Dropped by Amazon After OpenAI Partnership ‘Every Year After’ Renewed for Season 2 at Amazon; Will Focus on Charlie’s Story From ‘One Golden Summer’ Sequel Book ‘007 First Light’: Here’s Where to Buy the New James Bond Game Online Loading comments... title: BBC Buys War Drama ‘Kabul,’ Featuring Eric Dane in One of His Last Roles
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 3:15am PT BBC Buys War Drama ‘Kabul,’ Featuring Eric Dane in One of His Last Roles By K.J. Yossman Plus Icon K.J. Yossman @KJYossman Latest BBC Buys War Drama ‘Kabul,’ Featuring Eric Dane in One of His Last Roles 31 minutes ago ‘Burlesque the Musical’ London Opening Postponed to 2027, U.K. Tour Pushed Back (EXCLUSIVE) 21 hours ago International Cinema Union Criticizes Europe’s Approval of Paramount-Warner Bros. Merger: ‘It Has Based Its Decision on Too Narrow a Scope’ 22 hours ago See All Courtesy of BBC The BBC has acquired thriller series “Kabul,” starring the late Eric Dane and “Tehran’s” Shervin Alenabi, from Mediawan Rights. The six-part show , which is against the backdrop of U.S. troops’ withdrawal from Afghanistan amid the Taliban’s return to Kabul, follows the chaotic evacuation of various characters including diplomats, soldiers and civilians. The synopsis reads: “Kabul, August 15, 2021. As American troops withdraw, the Taliban enter the city. The Nazany family, like many civilians, is forced to flee, fearing retaliation. In this desperate and chaotic situation – shadowed by the looming threat of an attack by ISIS – French police officers, Italian diplomats, German military personnel, and American intelligence services must somehow coordinate to manage the influx of civilians. How will each of them manage to save their own lives? The countdown has begun.” Popular on Variety Related Stories Justin Baldoni Speaks Out on Blake Lively Legal Battle: 'There Have Been So Many Painful Things That Have Been Spoken Into Existence' Produced by France’s 24 25 Films and Cinétévé, it also stars Jonathan Zaccaï (“Le bureau des legendes”), Thibault Evrard (“The Night of the 12th”), Vassilis Kukalawi (“Kandahar”), Jeanne Goursaud (“Pax Massilia”), Gianmarco Saurino (“L’estate piu Calda”) and Valentina Cervi (“Medici: Masters of Florence”). The series is directed by Kasia Adamik (“No Escape”) and Olga Chajdas (“Imago”) from a screenplay by Olivier Demangel (“Atlantics,” “November”), Thomas Finkelkraut (“Thomas Finkielkraut”) and Joey Lavy (“The Shift”). “‘Kabul’ is a tragedy of the highest stakes,” said Nick Lee, the BBC’s head of program acquisition. “A warzone story which is propelled by the real ticking clock of the evacuation that was just five years ago this summer. Global politics remain complex, but ‘Kabul’ dramatises the biggest view of history through the smallest window of a human family.” Producers are Fabienne Servan-Schreiber and Charlotte Ortiz at Cinétévé, Matthias Weber and Thibault Gast at 24 25 Films (a Mediawan Company). Sandrine Paquot exec produces. The series was made in collaboration with the European Alliance with Mediawan Rights repping global sales. “Kabul” launches on the BBC’s streaming platform iPlayer on Aug. 2 and will be broadcast on BBC Four from Aug. 15. title: Casey Wasserman’s Exit Plan: New Owner of The Team May Be Same As Its Last One
full_text: × Skip to main content By Erik Hayden Plus Icon Erik Hayden Executive Editor, Business Erik_Hayden Follow Sign Up View All July 24, 2026 3:03am Casey Wasserman Andreas Rentz/Getty Images Casey Wasserman may soon be no longer on The Team. After being pressured to set up an auction for his Westwood-based firm that drew interest from United Talent Agency, Patrick Whitesell’s upstart shingle WIN and other suitors, the sale of his company may not go to any outside party. Wasserman is in talks to sell his stake in the company to private equity firm Providence Equity Partners, Matthew Belloni reported at Puck on Thursday. Providence had already taken a major stake in Wasserman in November 2022 to fuel growth at the company — notably, a major acquisition of Brillstein Entertainment less than a year later — so a buyout of the company’s founder would keep the firm in the same strategic hands it had been in for the past several years. The news of a would-be deal breakthrough arrives three months after the first round of bids were submitted in April to investment bank Moelis & Co, which handled the auction process. (The bank’s leader, Ken Moelis, sits on Wasserman’s LA28 Olympics Committee board). Providence, a source previously told The Hollywood Reporter , had put pressure on the mogul after a wave of headlines and an artist rebellion followed Wasserman being named in a tranche of Department of Justice documents related to convicted sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein and accomplice Ghislane Maxwell in late January. More than 20 artists said they were dropping Wasserman during the height of the February scandal — including Laufey, Chappell Roan, Best Coast and John Summit — and the firm’s social pages were deluged with calls for talent to distance themselves from the mogul. In an email to employees in February, Wasserman conceded “I have become a distraction” to the company’s business. Immediately following Wasserman’s surprise reveal at the time that he was selling his namesake firm, Providence issued a statement on Feb. 14 backing company president Mike Watts and saying, “We remain fully committed to investing in its growth, expanding its capabilities across sports, music, and entertainment, and supporting the extraordinary talent, brands, and properties the Company is proud to represent.” The DoJ documents showed that in 2003, Wasserman and Maxwell exchanged a series of flirtatious messages, though nothing criminal was mentioned in those emails. Wasserman had also, along with Bill Clinton, Chris Tucker and Kevin Spacey, flown on Epstein’s private jet on what was said to be a 2002 AIDS research trip to Africa. The mogul apologized at the time, saying on Jan. 31, “I deeply regret my correspondence with Ghislaine Maxwell which took place over two decades ago, long before her horrific crimes came to light. I never had a personal or business relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.” Wasserman — which rebranded as The Team in March in order to distance itself from its founder (it used to market itself as “Team Wass”) — is one of the biggest players in the sports space. As pro athletes have taken a greater share of the entertainment economy, sports is now one of the most sought after areas in the representation landscape. Wasserman’s sports division generated $266 million in revenue in 2024, 29 percent of the company’s total revenue, an S&P Global report from June of last year detailed. That’s second in Hollywood only to CAA’s sports division, which generated $578 million that year. Providence had invested in Wasserman after taking the place of prior investors RedBird Capital and Madrone Capital Partners. Those firms took ownership stakes in sports teams — football club AC Milan and the NFL’s Denver Broncos — and thus couldn’t own a stake in a sports talent representation firm. In addition to sports, The Team is also home to a notable music agency group built from its 2021 acquisition of Paradigm’s music division, as well as production-management firm Brillstein, a marketing services unit and more properties. Since founding Wasserman in 2002, the company has rolled up multiple creative firms as well as boutique agencies and had 4,000 employees as of earlier this year. As he navigates his exit from management, Wasserman also serves as the chair for LA28, which is organizing Los Angeles’ 2028 Olympics game plan. While city officials, including Mayor Karen Bass, have called upon Wasserman to step down from his high-profile role managing the games over the Maxwell emails, they have deferred to the LA28 board, which has backed Wasserman. In February, LA28 said it hired an outside law firm to investigate Wasserman’s correspondence with Maxwell and it found his “relationship with Epstein and Maxwell did not go beyond what has already been publicly documented.” THR

title: Sony Pictures Sets Viral X-Inspired Short-Form Thriller ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ at Prime Video Spain (EXCLUSIVE)
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 3:01am PT Sony Pictures Sets Viral X-Inspired Short-Form Thriller ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ at Prime Video Spain (EXCLUSIVE) The eight-part psychological thriller builds on a mobile-first pilot that generated 28.4 million views on TikTok By Emiliano de Pablos Plus Icon Emiliano de Pablos Latest Sony Pictures Sets Viral X-Inspired Short-Form Thriller ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ at Prime Video Spain (EXCLUSIVE) 43 minutes ago Studio STIM, Behind ‘The Girl in the Clouds,´Sets Canary Islands Operation, Planning to Create More Than 100 Jobs 1 day ago Spain’s SETT Invests $7.3 Million in New Canary Islands Animation Company Amuse Labs 1 week ago See All Courtesy of Sony Pictures International Productions Spain Sony Pictures International Productions’ Spanish operation is moving into short-form fiction with “Los Hilos del Miedo,” an eight-part psychological suspense series set to launch on Prime Video in Spain . Running approximately five minutes per episode, the production marks the division’s first short-form series and draws on stories by Spanish writer Salvador Gutiérrez Solís that went viral on X, captivating millions of readers across Spain and Latin America. Related Stories Justin Baldoni Speaks Out on Blake Lively Legal Battle: 'There Have Been So Many Painful Things That Have Been Spoken Into Existence' The series builds on “El Garaje,” a mobile-first pilot that generated 28.4 million total views, more than 262,000 likes and nearly 300,000 interactions on TikTok, according to Sony Pictures. Popular on Variety Inspired by the everyday-horror atmosphere of Gutiérrez Solís’ suspense stories, “Los Hilos del Miedo” is designed to translate digitally native storytelling into a premium audiovisual format while retaining the speed and immediacy of its social-media origins. The project also extends Sony Pictures International Productions’ collaboration with Madrid-based TAI Arts University School, integrating members of its international community of young creators into the series’ technical and artistic production. The partnership positions “Los Hilos del Miedo” as both a commercial production and a talent-development initiative, offering some TAI participants their first professional industry experience. “At Sony Pictures, we are always striving to challenge convention, and we are proud to be innovators in short-form entertainment and to premiere ‘Los Hilos del Miedo’ on Prime Video,” said Iván Losada, managing director of Sony Pictures Entertainment, Spain. Losada said the project demonstrates that “cinematic tension is not tied to traditional running times.” “Bringing Salvador Gutiérrez Solís’ massive viral reach on X into a five-minute episodic format allows us to bridge the gap between successful digital-native content and premium audiovisual production,” he said. Gutiérrez Solís, who publishes under the handle @gutisolis, described the adaptation as an unexpected leap for stories originally conceived for a social network. “It is especially exciting — almost unimaginable — to see these stories that I originally created for a social network end up on a platform with the global reach of Prime Video, alongside one of the most respected production companies in the audiovisual industry, Sony Pictures International Productions, Spain,” he said. The writer also highlighted the involvement of TAI’s young filmmakers, some of whom will gain their first professional experience on the series, and said the project could mark the beginning of a new generation of audiovisual creators. “Los Hilos del Miedo” will join Prime Video’s streaming catalogue in Spain at a date yet to be announced. title: Luxbox Acquires Documentary ‘Life of Jorge Luis Borges’ Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE)
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 2:34am PT Luxbox Acquires Documentary ‘Life of Jorge Luis Borges’ Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE) By Leo Barraclough Plus Icon Leo Barraclough International Features Editor LeoBarraclough Latest Luxbox Acquires Documentary ‘Life of Jorge Luis Borges’ Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE) 1 hour ago Venice Horizons Opener ‘The Girl With the Leica’ Boarded by Fandango Sales (EXCLUSIVE) 23 hours ago Venice-Bound Serbian Protest Drama ‘Until the Day Ends’ Boarded by Heretic (EXCLUSIVE) 23 hours ago See All Courtesy of El Pampero Cine Luxbox Films has acquired the world sales rights to Mariano Llinás ‘ epic five-and-a-half -hour documentary “Life of Jorge Luis Borges,” which will world premiere at the Venice Film Festival in the Open – Non-Fiction section. The film forms a biography of Argentine writer and poet Jorge Luis Borges, working from an exhaustive review of the papers he left, the books he read, and the places he went to. Related Stories Justin Baldoni Speaks Out on Blake Lively Legal Battle: 'There Have Been So Many Painful Things That Have Been Spoken Into Existence' The film is also “an attempt to decipher a very peculiar life: someone who lived like a hidden, underground warrior, whose cause was literature, literature conceived as a kingdom and as a landscape,” according to a statement. Popular on Variety “If Cold War spies fought from their desks or from an uninhabited house to defend their half of the world, Borges strove from a library to elevate and broaden the territories of his own tribe: the people for whom the most important part of the universe is books, plots and words.” Llinás is perhaps best known for 2018’s “La Flor,” which, ruling out experimental projects or mere cinematic exercises, is the longest film in the history of cinema. Before that, “Balnearios” (2002) and “Historias Extraordinarias” (2008) both had significant festival play. In 2003, he founded with Alejo Moguillansky, Agustín Mendilaharzu and Laura Citarella the group El Pampero Cine. The producers on “Life of Jorge Luis Borges” are Citarella, Ezequiel Pierr, Mendilaharzu, Moguillanksy and Llinás for El Pampero Cine. Llinás is the screenwriter. The DP is Mendilaharzu. The production designer is Ezequiel Pierri, and the editor is Ignacio Codino. Music is by Gabriel Chwojnik. The protagonists include Ernesto Montequín, Nicolás Helft, Laura Paredes, Milva Leonardi and Pablo Dacal. title: BBC Buys ‘Kabul’ Drama Series About American Withdrawal From Afghanistan Starring The Late Eric Dane
full_text: Skip to main content By Max Goldbart Max Goldbart International TV Co-Editor @Goldbart1 More Stories By Max BBC Buys ‘Kabul’ Drama Series About American Withdrawal From Afghanistan Starring The Late Eric Dane Microdrama Audiences Getting Younger & More Male, Holywater Tech Report Finds Hat Trick Productions Launches Live Comedy Arm View All July 24, 2026 2:21am L-R: Hannah Abdoh as Amina and Sara Taheri as Nooria. Domniki Mitropoulou - Cinétévé - 24 25 Films - France Télévisions The BBC has acquired the upcoming series Kabul set against the backdrop of the terrifying real-life events in Afghanistan from 2021, one of the last shows to star the late Eric Dane. The Mediawan drama for the European Alliance of broadcasters kicks off as American troops withdraw from Kabul and the Taliban enters the city. The Nazany family, like many civilians, is forced to flee, fearing retaliation. In this desperate and chaotic situation – shadowed by the looming threat of an attack by ISIS – French police officers, Italian diplomats, German military personnel, and American intelligence services must somehow coordinate to manage the influx of civilians. Related Stories News 'Ted Lasso' Star Toheeb Jimoh & 'Snow White's Dujonna Gift Leading BBC's 'Open Water' Adaptation News BBC Considers Locking Down iPlayer For Viewers Who Refuse To Pay License Fee Dane plays Martin. The supremely talented Euphoria and Grey’s Anatomy star died earlier this year . The international cast also includes Darina Al Joundi ( The New Look ), Shervin Alenabi ( Tehran ), Vassilis Koukalani ( Kandahar ), Hannah Abdoh, Jonathan Zaccaï ( Le bureau des legends ), Gianmarco Saurino ( L’estate piu Calda ) and Jeanne Goursaud ( Pax Massilia ). Watch on Deadline The show, which lands on the BBC next month, is produced by Mediawan and sold by Mediawan Rights is for the European Alliance of broadcasters which comprises ZDF, Rai and France Télévisions. The BBC isn’t technically part of the Alliance but has previously been involved with shows like the adaptation of Around the World in 80 Days. Nick Lee, Head of BBC Programme Acquisition, said: “ Kabul is a tragedy of the highest stakes. A warzone story which is propelled by the real ticking clock of the evacuation that was just five years ago this summer. Global politics remain complex, but Kabul dramatises the biggest view of history through the smallest window of a human family.” Kabul was directed by Kasia Adamik ( No Escape ) and Olga Chajdas ( Imago ) and written by Olivier Demangel ( Class Act ), Thomas Finkielkraut ( Baron Noir ) and Joey Lavy ( The Shift ). The series was produced by Fabienne Servan-Schreiber and Charlotte Ortiz at Cinétévé, Matthias Weber and Thibault Gast at 24 25 Films, and executive produced by Sandrine Paquot. Global sales are handled by Mediawan Rights. title: Jon Bon Jovi Ends Madison Square Garden Show Early Due to Sinus Infection: ‘We’ll Figure Out How to Reschedule’
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 2:05am PT Jon Bon Jovi Ends Madison Square Garden Show Early Due to Sinus Infection: ‘We’ll Figure Out How to Reschedule’ By Ellise Shafer Plus Icon Ellise Shafer Latest Jon Bon Jovi Ends Madison Square Garden Show Early Due to Sinus Infection: ‘We’ll Figure Out How to Reschedule’ 2 hours ago Venice Film Festival Lineup: Robert Pattinson’s ‘Primetime,’ Martin McDonagh’s ‘Wild Horse Nine,’ Oasis Reunion Doc and More 1 day ago Joel Courtney to Star in and Produce Thriller ‘Echo,’ TikTok Creator Zac Stracener’s Feature Directorial Debut (EXCLUSIVE) 2 days ago See All Getty Images for Bon Jovi Bon Jovi ‘s penultimate show of their Madison Square Garden residency came to an early end on Thursday night after frontman Jon Bon Jovi revealed he was suffering from a sinus infection. About 90 minutes into the set, Bon Jovi told the crowd that he would have to stop the show as he wasn’t feeling his best. “I haven’t talked to anybody about anything, but don’t throw away your ticket stubs,” Bon Jovi said, according to fan video . “I’m going to figure something out, OK? Just hold onto them, we’ll figure out how to reschedule. But I’m going to have to cool it for the night.” Related Stories Justin Baldoni Speaks Out on Blake Lively Legal Battle: 'There Have Been So Many Painful Things That Have Been Spoken Into Existence' He ended the show by saying, “I’ll see you again soon.” Popular on Variety A rep for the band told People : “Jon Bon Jovi spoke from stage and told fans he has been battling a sinus infection which led to the show’s early ending. As part of the Bon Jovi residency at Madison Square Garden, Jon has said numerous times that it has been a joy to return to live shows for the band. Updated information will be available shortly.” Bon Jovi underwent vocal cord surgery in 2022, which prevented frequent live performances. The “Forever” tour, which includes the Madison Square Garden shows as well as upcoming dates in the U.K. and Ireland, marks the band’s first major tour since his surgery. “I’m deeply grateful that the fans and the brotherhood of this band have been patient and allowed me the time needed to get healthy and prepare for touring,” Bon Jovi said when the tour was announced. Bon Jovi’s last Madison Square Garden show of the “Forever” tour is scheduled for Sunday, after which the band will head across the pond to play Edinburgh on Aug. 28, Dublin on Aug. 30 and two dates at London’s Wembley Stadium on Sept. 4 and 6. title: Christine Vachon, Ewan McGregor & Sarah Brocklehurst Among Names Set For Edinburgh Film Festival’s 2026 Industry Programme
full_text: Skip to main content By Zac Ntim Zac Ntim International Reporter @ZacharyNtim More Stories By Zac Christine Vachon, Ewan McGregor & Sarah Brocklehurst Among Names Set For Edinburgh Film Festival’s 2026 Industry Programme Taís Araujo To Star In ‘My Father’s Wives’ From Brazil’s Elo Studios & Portugal’s Bro Cinema Salaud Morisset Boards Ali Asgari’s ‘A Bit Of Light’ Ahead Of Venice Debut View All July 24, 2026 1:33am Edinburgh International Film Festival The Edinburgh Film Festival has confirmed the full lineup for the industry programme, which will run throughout the event’s 2026 edition, taking place from August 13 to 19. The programme will begin on August 14 with the EIFF Industry Networking Brunch in partnership with Women in Film and Television. This will be followed by an EIFF Industry Talk with veteran indie producer Christine Vachon hosted by journalist, moderator and film festival consultant Wendy Mitchell. The Vachon session will be followed by an on-stage EIFF In Conversation session with legendary documentary filmmaker Ken Burns hosted by journalist Dave Calhoun. Related Stories News Scottish Ballet & Forest Of Black Partner With Tull Stories To Release Dance Film 'These Violent Delights' -- First Look Trailer News 2026 Edinburgh International Film Festival: 10 Films To Debut In Competition; Kenneth Branagh To Receive Honorary Award The programme will continue with an EIFF In Conversation event, hosted in partnership with BAFTA Scotland, with Scottish actor Ewan McGregor. The first day will end with an EIFF Industry Talk titled ‘The Business of Film Roadshow,’ which will see leading industry experts sharing insights about the business side of the sector, hosted by Mia Bays, Director of the BFI Filmmaking Fund. Watch on Deadline The festival’s inaugural UK Film Conference begins on August 15. The event will start with an in-conversation session with British Film Institute CEO Ben Roberts and Clare Binns, who will discuss the future of independent film in the UK. The UK Film Conference continues with ‘Low Budget, High Ambition’, a session focusing on low-budget filmmaking and how UK producers are adapting to an ever-changing landscape. That will be followed by the EIFF Industry session ‘Would This Get Made?’, an innovative concept where industry professionals will be quizzed on how they would approach the production of a range of projects from the past if they came across their desks in 2026. Speakers include producer Christine Vachon, Stefanie Fahrion (Film4), Alice Ojha (BBC Film), Laura Wilson (Altitude) and Simon Renshaw (Casarotto Ramsay & Associates). The session will be hosted by Wendy Mitchell. The EIFF Industry Talk session ‘New Nightmares’ will conclude the UK Film Conference with speakers discussing the current state of genre work embracing its moment. Speakers include actor and filmmaker Max Minghella (The Handmaid’s Tale, Clayface) and filmmakers Prano Bailey- Bond (Censor), Chee Keong Cheung (Bad Day at the Office) and Joby Stephens (Abandoned). Hosted by horror content creator Chris Mayo (Big Fat Cult). Elsewhere, Bruce Dern will take part in a career Q&A with film critic Guy Lodge and documentary filmmaker Paul Sng will host a session titled ‘Unmaking the Colonial Gaze in Cinema.’ Discussing this year’s industry lineup, EIFF CEO and Festival Director, Paul Ridd said: “We could not be prouder of this year’s Industry Programme at EIFF which offers up a diverse and starry range of guests, talks, lively discussions and networking opportunities for Scottish, UK and international folk looking to make connections and hear from some leading experts in their fields, all more accessible than ever before. Our inaugural UK Film Conference is something of the jewel in our crown, but there are so many more must-attend events elsewhere in the programme, all encompassing our fundamental mission to be the home of independent cinema in the UK.” Judge Extends TRO, Pausing Paramount-WBD Merger By Another Two Weeks Surprise Depp Visit As Scrooge For ‘Ebenezer’; Thursday Photo Gallery; Panels Atari, Universal Ink Film Deal For ‘Missile Command,’ ‘Asteroids’, Other Classic Games Lineup, Talking Points, Q&A With Festival Chief & More From Lido Read More About: Comments

title: Hong Kong International Film Festival Society Appoints Fred Tsui As Executive Director
full_text: Skip to main content By Liz Shackleton Liz Shackleton pmc-media-editor-2024 More Stories By Liz Michelle Zhou’s Venice Horizons Title ‘Big Little Things’ Boarded By Moebius Entertainment For Sales Billkin Putthipong To Star In GDH’s ‘Untitled Snooker Project’, Produced By Team Behind ‘Gohan’ & ‘How To Make Millions Before Grandma Dies’ Edko Films Picks Up World Rights To Yeung Leung Chuen’s Toronto Discovery Title ‘We Are Born Good’ View All July 24, 2026 12:49am Fred Tsui Hong Kong International Film Festival Society Hong Kong International Film Festival Society (HKIFFS) has announced that veteran sales agent and producer Fred Tsui will succeed Albert Lee as executive director of the Hong Kong International Film Festival Society. In his new role, commencing on September 23, Tsui will oversee the Hong Kong International Film Festival and its year-round activities, the Hong Kong-Asia Film Financing Forum (HAF), HAF Film Lab, Summer International Film Festival, and the HKIFF Cine Fan programme. He will report to HKIFFS chairman Wilfred Wong and the Board of Directors. Related Stories News Hong Kong International Film Festival To Open With Anthony Chen's 'We Are All Strangers' & Closing With Philip Yung's 'Cyclone' News Asian Film Awards Pauses 2026 Competitive Categories & Red Carpet Tsui has worked across nearly every sector of the film industry, from film sales, co-production and financing to distribution, exhibition, broadcasting and media management – building extensive international networks among filmmakers and industry execs. Watch on Deadline In his most recent role, Tsui has established sales agency and festival consultancy company Moebius Entertainment, which recently picked up Venice Horizons title Big Little Things and Toronto Special Presentations title Happily Ever After . Prior to Moebius, Tsui spent more than twenty years at Media Asia Film, where he served as General Manager and Head of International Sales & Co-Production, helping the studio expand internationally and position Hong Kong cinema on the world stage. Earlier in his career, Tsui held senior management positions at PCCW and Asia Television, where he developed expertise in organisational leadership, media operations, strategic planning and content management. Tsui also serves as a member of the Hong Kong Film Development Council and has been invited to serve on juries at leading international film festivals, including Hong Kong, Edinburgh, Jecheon, Tallinn, Seattle, Udine, Sitges, Sonoma and Skip City in Japan. He is a member of the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences (AMPAS), Hong Kong Film Critics Society, and has previously served on the Board of Directors of the Independent Film & Television Alliance (IFTA) in the U.S., as well as the board of the MIA International Audiovisual Market in Rome. “We are delighted to welcome Fred to HKIFFS,” said HKIFFS chairman Wilfred Wong. “Fred combines strategic leadership with an exceptional understanding of the international film landscape. Throughout his career, he has built bridges between Hong Kong cinema and the global marketplace while earning the trust and respect of filmmakers, festival directors and industry leaders worldwide. “Just as importantly, he approaches cinema from the perspectives of an executive, a critic, a festival juror and a lifelong cinephile. We believe he is uniquely qualified to lead HKIFFS into its next chapter, further strengthening its role as one of Asia’s most important cultural institutions and international film platforms.” Tsui said: “It is a tremendous honour to join HKIFFS, an institution that has played such a pivotal role in the cultural life of Hong Kong and in promoting Asian cinema internationally. “Having worked with film festivals throughout my career—as a sales executive, producer, juror, critic and audience member – I have seen first-hand the unique role HKIFF plays in connecting filmmakers, the industry and audiences. I look forward to working with the Board, our partners and the HKIFFS team to build on the Society’s remarkable legacy while broadening its international reach, nurturing new talent, deepening audience engagement and ensuring Hong Kong remains at the forefront of global film culture.” Judge Extends TRO, Pausing Paramount-WBD Merger By Another Two Weeks Surprise Depp Visit As Scrooge For ‘Ebenezer’; Thursday Photo Gallery; Panels Atari, Universal Ink Film Deal For ‘Missile Command,’ ‘Asteroids’, Other Classic Games Lineup, Talking Points, Q&A With Festival Chief & More From Lido Read More About: Comments

title: ‘Jumbo’ Sequel, ‘Queen of Malacca’ Franchise Plans on the Cards as Indonesia’s Visinema Eyes Expansion (EXCLUSIVE)
full_text: Jul 24, 2026 12:02am PT ‘Jumbo’ Sequel, ‘Queen of Malacca’ Franchise Plans on the Cards as Indonesia’s Visinema Eyes Expansion (EXCLUSIVE) By Lin Ying-Hsuan Plus Icon Lin Ying-Hsuan Latest Anurag Kashyap, MIFFest Jury President, Searches for ‘a New Cinematic Language’ 3 days ago APOS 2026 Takeaways: IP, Sport, AI Define Asia’s New Content Era 1 month ago ReelShort Looks Beyond Microdramas as Joey Jia Pushes for a New Entertainment Ecosystem 1 month ago See All MIFFest Indonesian filmmaker and Visinema founder Angga Dwimas Sasongko is targeting the first quarter of 2027 for the release of “ Queen of Malacca ,” as the Southeast Asian fantasy epic moves through post-production and attracts interest from potential distribution partners in China, Thailand and other international markets. The project has drawn growing industry attention since Blue Finch Films boarded worldwide sales . “We are currently in discussions with potential distribution partners,” Sasongko tells Variety during the Malaysia International Film Festival ( MIFFest ) in Kuala Lumpur. “It will definitely become the next franchise for Visinema.” Related Stories Sky Yang Signs With Range Media Partners (EXCLUSIVE) “Queen of Malacca” marks Sasongko’s return to directing after a two-year break and is one of the most ambitious productions of his career. “I spent eight years building this fantasy world,” he says. “It combines Southeast Asian martial arts, supernatural elements and extensive visual effects. It’s like creating a universe – a mosaic of modern life inspired by regional mythology and traditions.” Popular on Variety More than a standalone feature, Sasongko sees “Queen of Malacca” as the cornerstone of Visinema’s strategy to develop locally rooted IP with international appeal. Beyond “Queen of Malacca,” Visinema is preparing five films for release in 2027 while developing an animation pipeline extending through 2030. The slate includes a superhero action-comedy animated feature slated for 2028, a sequel to the family IP “Nussa” and a follow-up to its animated blockbuster “ Jumbo ,” one of Indonesia’s biggest box office hits of all time. “For Visinema, international collaboration isn’t simply about securing more financing – it’s about expanding together,” Sasongko says. “Since 2018, we’ve generally limited external investment in our projects to below 40%, because we look at what strategic partners can contribute beyond capital.” Those partners include media companies, foreign producers and distributors that can provide market access, industry expertise and long-term strategic value. Visinema is also looking to deepen relationships across mainland China, Thailand, Vietnam and the Philippines. For Sasongko, creative ambition and commercial potential should go hand in hand. The goal, he says, is to build stories with a distinctive local identity while creating the partnerships and long-term IP needed to reach audiences across the region. title: Chris Affleck, Mother of Oscar Winners Ben and Casey Affleck, Dies at 83
full_text: Skip to main content By McKinley Franklin Plus Icon McKinley Franklin View All July 23, 2026 9:21pm Chris Anne Affleck and Ben Affleck. Getty Chris Anne Affleck, the mother of Oscar winners Ben Affleck and Casey Affleck , died June 2, her family announced . She was 83 and diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in December. Christine Anne Boldt was born in New York City in December 1942. She was a graduate of Harvard University and spent 35 years as a public school teacher. She retired in 2008 and was a fierce civil rights advocate throughout her life, having served as one of the original freedom riders who went into the Deep South during the 1960s. Chris and her ex-husband Timothy Affleck welcomed their first child, Ben, in 1972. Ben’s brother, Casey, was born in 1975, around the time their family relocated to Massachusetts, where she continued her teaching career. Related Stories TV Mary Kate Golding, 'The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel' Set Decorator, Dies at 34 in Stabbing Lifestyle Anne Russell, Actress in 'Hello, Dolly!' and 'Mame,' Dies at 103 “My mother taught public school, went to Harvard and then got her master’s there and taught fifth and sixth grade in a public school,” Ben told The Hollywood Reporter in 2012. “My dad had a more working-class lifestyle. He didn’t go to college. He was an auto mechanic and a bartender and a janitor at Harvard.” By the time Ben was 12, his parents divorced. As his passion for entertainment grew during his adolescence, Chris played a hand in his early industry beginnings, connecting her son with her former Harvard roommate, casting director Patty Collins, who helped him audition for commercials and TV movies. From left: Chris Affleck, Ben Affleck, Matt Damon and his mother, Nancy Carlson-Paige, at the 70th Academy Awards. Jim Smeal/Ron Galella Collection via Getty Images She was an avid supporter of her sons’ acting careers and attended the 1998 Oscars alongside Ben and Matt Damon, who also brought his mother as his date, after the pair were nominated for best original screenplay for Good Will Hunting — a film the duo also starred in alongside Casey. Ben and Damon would go on to win the Oscar, and the film has become a classic. “We were sitting next to our moms and we won, and we kind of hugged our moms. I remember how everyone had made such a big thing out of it as if this was such a novelty,” Ben told Vanity Fair of the moment. “I remember thinking, being insecure, like, ‘Why? Why is it weird that we’re bringing our moms?’ Who else do you think we would bring?” Casey Affleck and his mom attended the ‘Committed’ premiere in April 2000. Ron Galella, Ltd./Ron Galella Collection via Getty Images Ben would go on to win his second Oscar, for best picture, for Argo in 2012. Casey would win the best actor Oscar in 2017 for his performance in Kenneth Lonergan’s Manchester by the Sea . Chris was given six months to live in December, and her greatest wish after her diagnosis was seeing grandson Atticus graduate high school, her family said. She attended the event with her family on May 31 and died peacefully in her sleep two days later. In addition to her sons, survivors include her five grandchildren: Casey’s kids, Atticus and Indiana, and Ben’s kids, Violet, Fin and Sam. THR

title: Jon Bon Jovi Abruptly Ends MSG Show Amid Vocal Issues, Will Reschedule: “You’re Not Getting The Best Of Me”
full_text: Skip to main content By Glenn Garner Glenn Garner Associate Editor @xoxo_glenn More Stories By Glenn Cole Hauser On “Challenges” Of Making ‘Dutton Ranch’ Without Taylor Sheridan: “Everything Had Changed” John Leguizamo “Could Not See” For 12 To 14 Hours A Day Playing Blind ‘The Odyssey’ Role ‘Obsession’s Michael Johnston Wants To Play “Intentional, Meaningful Queer Role”: “Maybe I’ll Write It Myself” View All July 23, 2026 9:11pm Jon Bon Jovi performs onstage at the Forever Tour kick-off on July 7, 2026 at Madison Square Garden in New York City. Just hold onto it, we’ll figure out how to reschedule,” added Bon Jovi. “But I’m gonna have to cool it for the night.” The lead singer previously underwent reconstructive surgery on his vocal cords in 2022 after issues with his voice since 2015, which he opened up about in the Hulu docuseries Thank You, Goodnight: The Bon Jovi Story . title: Charli XCX Brings in the Post-‘Brat’ Era With ‘Music, Fashion, Film’
full_text: × Skip to main content By Ethan Millman Plus Icon Ethan Millman Music Editor View All July 23, 2026 9:00pm Charli xcx attends the 2026 Met Gala celebrating "Costume Art" at the Metropolitan Museum of Art on May 04, 2026 in New York City. (Photo by Jamie McCarthy/Getty Images) Following much anticipation, Charli XCX is officially back with Music , Fashion, Film , her seventh album overall and her first since her seminal Brat took over the world in 2024. While she hasn’t technically released a solo album since then, pop’s most trendsetting auteur has been certifiably, emphatically, Not On A Break. “The second people are getting sick of you, that’s when you have to go even harder,” Kylie Jenner, playing a fictionalized version of herself, told Charli in one of the most-memorable parts of her mockumentary film The Moment , which came out back in January. Whether that was meant to be delivered with sincerity or irony, it certainly carries some level of poignancy of how Charli has operated since Brat summer started. Related Stories Movies Venice Chief Alberto Barbera on Hollywood's Festival Retreat, Gender Parity Backslide and the AI Question TV 'A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms' Creator Gets Candid on Season 2: "It's Been a F***ing Bastard" She released Brat’ s deluxe version three days after the original album drop, then the Brat and It’s Completely Different but Also Still Brat remix album in October. She embarked on two headlining arena tours — the Sweat Tour with Troye Sivan, and her eponymous Brat Tour. She’s had roles in seven different feature films: The Moment, where she starred and produced, as well as 100 Nights of Hero , Sacrifice , Erupcja , I Want Your Sex, The Gallerist and Faces of Death. She also created the soundtrack for Wuthering Heights and co-wrote the soundtrack for the Anne Hathaway-starring Mother Mary with Jack Antonoff. That exhaustive resume alone certainly puts Charli firmly in contention as one of Hollywood’s hardest working stars, and with Music, Fashion, Film officially here, that’s not stopping any time soon. Charli is ramping up for another extensive tour, with festival headlining dates at Lollapalooza, Outside Lands and Reading and Leeds over the next month, then more arena dates starting in the fall. “I was really, really ready to peace out and take a break and not make music,” she told Rolling Stone in the magazine’s June cover story. “I felt very creatively drained and not inspired to write anything new. Then suddenly, inspiration came.” Charli started the Music, Fashion, Film era seeking to distance herself from the dance floors where Brat held court, declaring that “the dance floor is dead” on the more polarizing lead single “Rock Music.” She followed that with the vibey, synth-y “SS26,” the equally summery, cheekier “Wink Wink,” and most-recent single “Camera,” which featured a music video starring Vincent Cassel. With Music, Fashion, Film firmly in focus, the next few months present an interesting chapter into how Charli enters her most-anticipated era to date. Brat turned Charli XCX from niche iconoclast to mainstream icon. It’d be tempting to milk that well dry — it’s hard to imagine any artist replicating the moment Charli created with Brat anytime soon — but she’s never been one to dwell too long on the past with her work. As she told Rolling Stone in June: “I knew when I was making it that I was never going to make that record again. It’s not creatively rewarding for me to make the same thing twice.” THR

title: Director Na Hong-Jin Makes Comic-Con Debut With Frightening New ‘Hope’ Footage, Says Casting Hoyeon Was His ‘Best Decision’
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 8:27pm PT Director Na Hong-Jin Makes Comic-Con Debut With Frightening New ‘Hope’ Footage, Says Casting Hoyeon Was His ‘Best Decision’ By Angelique Jackson Plus Icon Angelique Jackson angelique814 Latest Director Na Hong-Jin Makes Comic-Con Debut With Frightening New ‘Hope’ Footage, Says Casting Hoyeon Was His ‘Best Decision’ 6 hours ago Johnny Depp Makes Surprise Comic-Con Appearance as Ebenezer Scrooge 12 hours ago The Roku Channel Expands Partnership With Create the Writers Room Initiative by Streaming ‘Five Film Fridays’ Selections 18 hours ago See All Getty Images South Korean director Na Hong-Jin made his San Diego Comic-Con debut to preview his latest film, Neon’s alien action movie “ Hope .” Before the action auteur took the stage in Ballroom 20, a sizzle reel of his previous work — from his 2008 debut “The Chaser” to “The Yellow Sea” and “The Wailing,” all set to Ozzy Osbourne’s “Crazy Train” — played for the rapt audience. Related Stories Sky Yang Signs With Range Media Partners (EXCLUSIVE) “Hope,” Na said, “is a continuation in terms of the same metaphor and the same meaning, but in terms of the style and the thought and the content is completely different. Any assumptions you have about my premises and my previous work, I would advise them to completely abandon it before [this one].” “Hope” is set in the remote village of Hope Harbor, where a mysterious creature has wreaked havoc. Police and local hunters set out to track the beast and find themselves hunted instead. But not all is as it seems. Popular on Variety The alien action movie stars Hoyeon , who broke out internationally thanks to her Emmy-nominated turn in Netflix’s “Squid Game.” Na said was one of the best decisions he made in bringing the film, a 10 year passion project, to the big screen. Hwang Jung-min, who leads the international cast, recommended Hoyeon for the role of a tenacious police officer. “He said that if you meet her, you realize how special of a person she is,” Na said, explaining that he immediately set up a meeting. The director and star read the script while eating takeout and she quickly signed on for the role. “The funny thing is: Hwang Jung-min actually has never met her, or didn’t even know her very well.” Hoyeon appeared in a special video message to introduce two clips that would play exclusively for the Comic-Con crowd. The first is a tense sequence where Hwang as he and a group of men shoot their way through the wreckage of their town — only to finally come face to face with the gigantic alien preying upon them. The second was an extended look at a chase scene where one character played by must leap from his horse into the police car — all while being chased down the highway by one of the fanged creatures. After the pulse-pounding clips ended, director Na revealed that these weren’t the most difficult scenes to shoot: That honor belongs to another chase scene with the characters fleeing through the forest on horseback. Na also praised Hoeyon’s performance in another scene and shared she spent six months learning to drive a manual stick-shift car. “After six months, she reached a point where she could perform those drafts at very different, difficult angles too,” he said. “So she was driving the car and doing all the performance herself, and I think the word ‘difficult’ is not enough to describe what was going on in that scene. My DP and I almost died of anxiety just watching her.” Speaking of dying, during the Q&A portion of the panel, Na teased that “90%” of the characters in the film might perish… but he won’t reveal who. The exchange got a roar of laughter from the crowd. “Hope,” which also stars Michael Fassbender, Alicia Vikander, Taylor Russell and Cameron Britton, premiered in competition at this year’s Cannes Film Festival, where it electrified audiences. Neon hopes to replicate that same success when releasing the film in the U.S. on Sept. 9. In Cannes, Na revealed that he had plans for a sequel. “I think you can readily imagine this sequel. And there’s a script that’s already been done that I’d like to shoot,” he said during his Cannes press conference. “So if I have the opportunity, I would indeed make a sequel if possible.” Carole Radziwill Confronted About Jeffrey Epstein Connection in ‘RHONY’ Season 16 Trailer: ‘You Think I’m Part of an International Sex-Trafficking Ring?’ The Pulitzer Prize-Winning Journalist Who Has the Entire NBA Spooked Loading comments... title: ‘Sia: Nostalgic for the Present’ Review: A Concert Performance Film from 2016, but It Channels Sia’s Mystery and Power
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 8:25pm PT ‘Sia: Nostalgic for the Present’ Review: A Concert Performance Film from 2016, but It Channels Sia’s Mystery and Power Made for her tour from a decade ago, it's a dance-video projection of Sia's greatest hits. It makes you wish that she'd craft another one. By Owen Gleiberman Plus Icon Owen Gleiberman Chief Film Critic @OwenGleiberman Latest ‘Sia: Nostalgic for the Present’ Review: A Concert Performance Film from 2016, but It Channels Sia’s Mystery and Power 7 hours ago ‘Summer Tour’ Review: A Documentary About Deadheads That’s as Blissed-Out and Navel-Gazing as Its Subjects 3 days ago ‘Manhunter: The Final Cut’ Shows Why Director’s Cuts Are Almost Never an Improvement. But It’s Still the Greatest Thriller of Our Time 5 days ago See All Courtesy of Trafalgar Releasing When Lady Gaga first ascended to supernova celebrity, with the release of “The Fame” in 2008, there was a time when she kept her face hidden — under the elaborate masks she wore in videos, and even in interviews, for which she would also show up masked. It was a highly elaborate act of camouflage, but the ruse worked. She was only 22, yet she created an aura of mystery around herself, conjuring “Lady Gaga” as a larger-than-life character. (When you heard that she was really Stefani Germanotta from New York City, you would think: Really, who is that?) Related Stories Sky Yang Signs With Range Media Partners (EXCLUSIVE) Sia , born in Australia 10 years before Lady Gaga, had a career that predated hers as well. But by the time Sia hit her solo stride in the late 2000s, she didn’t simply borrow from the Gaga mask playbook. She concealed her face to the point that the mystery became part of who she was. “ Sia: Nostalgic for the Present ,” which opens tomorrow and is showing in select theaters for a limited run, is a performance film that was originally created for Sia’s 2016 Nostalgic for the Present tour, and in the entire movie you never once see Sia’s face. Popular on Variety She’s right there in front of us, standing and singing on a platform at stage left, hidden under a half-blonde, half-black wig with bangs that hang down to her lips. There’s an oversize white bow on top of the wig, and she wears a white dress that resembles a nurse’s uniform from the early ’60s. Sia’s greatest songs, like “Unstoppable” and “Titanium” and “The Greatest,” are anthems of strength, power, fortitude, invincibility, and watching the movie it’s fascinating to behold how her facelessness feeds that quality. She’s an abstract figure, a pair of lips singing, and this creates the sensation that the power she’s singing about doesn’t emanate from her. She’s channeling a force larger than herself — the rising specter of feminine triumph. She’s merely the vessel. I’m not entirely sure what the two-tone wig means (the duality of woman?), but everyone in the movie wears a version of it: the dancers, the actors (Kristen Wiig, Paul Dano, Ben Mendelsohn, Gaby Hoffman) who each appear in one dance video, as well as the choreographer, costume designer, and offstage crew members. They’re all wearing the wig, which looks like the fabled New York black-and-white cookie turned into a fashion emblem of talismanic power. “Sia: Nostalgic for the Present” is basically a 75-minute-long series of dance videos that were designed to be projected onstage during the tour, as the live performers mirrored the movements in the videos. The featured star is, of course, Maddie Ziegler, the whirling dervish of a child dancer who pranced and flung and gyrated her way through three celebrated Sia music videos; she was 14 when the Nostalgic for the Present tour unfolded. This weekend, if you go to “Sia: Nostalgic for the Present,” what you’re seeing is a 10-year-old movie (and a promotional one at that), so it’s severely lacking in the shock of the new. There’s no getting around the fact that it’s a greatest-hits compendium. But though Sia’s last major hit was a decade ago, she had such an intuitive gift for crafting powerfully infectious pop songs that it’s hard to understand why she hit a wall. I think it might have had something to do with the mask/wig/hiding thing. Pop stars, especially the ones who have extended careers, are people who have a primal desire to put themselves out there. Whereas Sia clearly had a primal ambivalence about that. She was a camera-shy exhibitionist. In “Nostalgic for the Present,” her voice is commanding, ringing out with those soul inflections that make her sound like Amy Winehouse if Winehouse were being produced by Giorgio Moroder. The dance performance that’s at the heart of the film is the sort of thing that felt transgressive back then, but now it looks like something out of a 2010s nostalgia reel. Maddie Ziegler, who was born in Pittsburgh and got her start on “Dance Moms,” flings her body around with a kind of airy gymnastic aggression, and she specializes in facial contortions meant to express the underlying ferocity of Sia’s music (and are sometimes just there as psycho flourishes to mess with the audience). Ziegler is like a mime crossed with Daryl Hannah in “Blade Runner,” but there’s also a touch of Sid Vicious to the way that she’ll mug and curl her lips like a teenage brat who’s gone off her meds. The hits remain sublime. I retain a special adoration for “Chandelier,” a song of hedonistic assertion and underlying ambivalence that, for me, is filtered through its brilliant use as the centerpiece of the Broadway version of “Moulin Rouge!” I’m writing about Sia as if she had gone away, but as someone who loves her music, I see no reason on earth why she couldn’t now enjoy a comeback as ferocious as the one that Gaga had with “Abracadabra.” She wouldn’t even need to come out from behind the wig; that would play like gangbusters on the Grammys. But she’d have to rouse herself to do what she used to do: create a dance hook as shiny and fierce and glorious as titanium. ‘Sia: Nostalgic for the Present’ Review: A Concert Performance Film from 2016, but It Channels Sia’s Mystery and Power Reviewed at Regal Union Square, New York, July 23, 2026. Executive producers: Sia, Jonathan Daniel, David Russell, Jennifer Heath, Daniel Askill, Sara Greco. Crew: Directors: Sia, Daniel Askill. Camera: Bobby Shore. Editor: Lorin Askill. Music: Sia. With: Sia, Maddie Ziegler, Nick Lanzisera, Stephanie Mincone, Wyatt Rocker, Paul Dano, Gaby Hoffman, Ben Mendelsohn, Tig Notaro, Kristen Wiig. title: ‘Resident Evil’s Zach Cregger & Austin Abrams Tease Film’s Last 30 Seconds, Tricky Camerawork & Potential Fan Favorite Set Pieces
full_text: Skip to main content By Destiny Jackson Destiny Jackson Crafts Editor, Awards @DestinyDreadful More Stories By Destiny USC Games Expo Shaping Next Generation Of Game Creators Through ‘Aftertaste’, ‘Sisyphus’s Worst Day (Again)’ & ‘Unrealtor’ ‘Marvel’s Wolverine’ Video Game Unpacks Formidable Foes, Jean Grey Relationship & Leaning Into Logan’s “Trauma” At Comic-Con Shailene Woodley Talks ‘Paradise’ Emmy Nomination, “No Drama” Work Environments & Teases ‘Count My Lies’ Adaptation View All July 23, 2026 8:17pm Austin Abrams in 'Resident Evil' Columbia Pictures It may be “no sweat” for Austin Abrams ’ character Bryan in the latest trailer for Resident Evil , but for writer-director and horror aficionado Zach Cregger , his upcoming adaptation is all blood, all guts and all gory. Co-written with Shay Hatten, Sony’s Resident Evil follows Bryan, a medical courier tasked with delivering an important package to the infamous in-game dwellings of Raccoon City. However, when Bryan arrives, he finds himself in a nonstop race for survival as the treacherous night unfolds around him. The film marks the latest big-screen adaptation of Resident Evil , the popular horror video game series from Capcom, which also doubles as one of the highest-grossing video game franchises ever. Related Stories News Zach Cregger Recalls 'Resident Evil' Near On-Set Accident That "Would've Killed" Austin Abrams Trailers 'Resident Evil' Trailer: Zach Cregger's Take On Iconic Video Game Is Coming For You (And Comic-Con) While attending the Resident Evil x Discord Fan Event at Comic-Con celebrating the film’s impending September 18 release, Cregger and Abrams spoke to Deadline about the intimate fan-loving details that went into bringing the horror game to life. Watch on Deadline DEADLINE: What about Zach Cregger made you want to open yourself up to the large, passionate Resident Evil fanbase with this film? AUSTIN ABRAMS: For me, it was Zach doing his own thing with this series. I just loved working with him so much. I knew that even though it was Resident Evil , I was excited and not nervous because he loved Resident Evil . He’s played so much of it that I felt I can really trust him. It was very much about trusting him and being like, “Let’s do fucking whatever, let’s make some shit.” DEADLINE: What kind of fears is Bryan experiencing? In the latest trailer, he seems to be having some relationship troubles. What kind of things did you keep in mind while playing this character? ABRAMS: Yeah, he is dealing with relationship troubles, and then that’s juxtaposed with immediately having to survive and fight for his life, which I think makes him have to look at what he’s so afraid of in his regular day and in his relationship with fresh eyes. ZACH CREGGER: I think of Brian as someone who is just starting his adult life. He has a job, but not a career. He’s got a relationship that he really loves, but there’s a crisis in that relationship and he feels like he’s in a horror movie of his own when we start. His girlfriend is pregnant, he’s not ready. He’s thinking, “What is my life going to be?” And he feels like he’s in his own little horror movie, and then all of a sudden, a real horror movie interrupts him and it puts all of that into perspective. DEADLINE: What was the non-negotiable essential to preserving the taste of the Resident Evil franchise in the film? CREGGER: Well, there’s a couple of things you have to have. First, you have the source of all the chaos of the T-virus, which is the thing that was developed by the Umbrella corporation. If you don’t do that, for me, it’s not Resident Evil . I feel like setting it in Raccoon City on the eve of the viral outbreak felt like, “OK, that’s me laying the table.” But to me, the most important thing to make it a Resident Evil story is the tone and the rhythm. It’s like this kind of slow progression deeper and deeper into hell. And in the first act of the movie, he’s creeping into darkness, and then at a certain point, he’s running for his life. It sounds like an action movie, but it’s not. Resident Evil is a survival horror, not a survival action. He’s not a badass going around and karate kicking these zombies. It’s more intense, grounded dread, tone and atmosphere. One of the things those games do so well is that they lay out such a distinct and effective atmosphere. And so, I feel like to me, what makes this a real love letter to those games is I’m trying to replicate that experience of feeling dread and tension. DEADLINE: Now Zach, this movie is only 95 minutes. You didn’t want to make a longer movie or more movies? CREGGER: I think movies are too long, dude. I’ve got a dog at home, and if I go to a movie, I sit through a half hour of trailers and then the movie, and I’m driving back home. I’m half-joking, but I think it was just a challenge to tell a really efficient story without any extra calories. I wanted to be lean and mean and tight. DEADLINE: Were there fan favorites like Lady Dimitrescu or Wesker or other easter eggs that landed on the cutting room floor as a result of the tightness? CREGGER: I intentionally didn’t want to put any of the characters in the games into the movie because what I don’t want to do is cannibalize all these things that the games do so well. It’s kind of like when you read your favorite book and then you realize they’re going to turn it into a movie, and then they cast someone you don’t agree with, and you’re just like, it hurts. I love those games. I don’t want to step on their toes. I want to celebrate those games, and I want to make something that other people who love the games like me are going to recognize as Resident Evil . But I certainly don’t think I’m going to do a better job of representing someone like Leon than the games. DEADLINE: Was there a particular shot or set piece that made you really feel like, “Oh yeah, this is the movie I want to make and what I want audiences to see”? CREGGER: The Jump Street sequence that’s in the trailer where he’s running down the street and these zombies are leaping off the buildings while things are exploding all around him — we did it practically and it was super intense. I was really proud of what we did there. ABRAMS: That one for me too. CREGGER: But then, there’s also the first half hour. It’s short like one big long scene in a way that I think works so well. Then the last 30 seconds of the movie to me are so—I’m very proud of the last 30 seconds. So when I watch that, when the movie ends, I’m always really happy. DEADLINE: Resident Evil is known for resource management and making impossible choices. Were there any filmmaking choices that mirrored this dynamic? CREGGER: Well, resource management is every movie ever made. We have almost enough to do what we want. How do we make it work? So yeah, every scene, you’re kind of like, “How is this possible?” But impossible choices, no. I mean, no, you kind of have to just decide what you’re going to do and lock in and make it happen. DEADLINE: In bringing this game to life, were there any moments for you as an actor that really made you break a sweat? ABRAMS: We were doing a lot of camera work. It was a real dance with the camera. James [Van Der Reyden], our camera operator, had to be really precise, and I had to be really precise to his shots and come around to the camera at the same time. It was about forming a language. CREGGER: Yeah. The reason for that is because in the video games, you’re following your avatar [because it’s a third-person game], so when you look left the camera swings left, and when you look right the camera swings to look right. I wanted to do that also with the movie, which means Austin and James have to be in lockstep. When he goes up the stairs, we look at the stairs, in the kitchen, we look at the kitchen and then back to another thing. So that was fun to dial in, and I think it works well. DEADLINE: Are there any easter egg references that you’re especially excited for longtime fans to see come to life? I know in the trailer there were some herbs and a typewriter… CREGGER: Yes, there’s a lot of objects directly from the game, like the ones you mentioned. And locks and padlocks play a big role in the movie. He’s constantly getting stopped by locks, which is a thing in the games. I think that’s the thing that fans are going to recognize beyond overt one for one references, is the flow of the movie is the same as the flow of the game. You’re moving from one set piece to set piece. And each set piece contains its own unique threat. And I think that’s what the games do well. It’s a naturally cinematic rhythm. So it was fun to port the DNA of the games in terms of their story, pacing and into the movie. title: Brad Bird Thinks ‘1906’ Would “Lend Itself” To Multiple Episodes As TV Miniseries About S.F. Earthquake
full_text: Skip to main content By Dessi Gomez Dessi Gomez SEO Staff Writer @GomezDessi More Stories By Dessi Rebelle Media & MGM Television Win Rights To Adapt ‘When In Rome’ Series By Sarah Adams Audiobook Narrator Jeff Hays Joins Peacock’s ‘Dungeon Crawler Carl’ TV Series To Voice Princess Donut The Cat ‘Ransom Canyon’ Showrunner Breaks Down Season 2: Grift, Love Triangles, Surprise Pregnancy & Ending That Is “Launch Of Next Story” For Possible Season 3 View All July 23, 2026 7:38pm Brad Bird speaks at the 'Brad Bird: My Cinematic Journey' Hall H panel during 2026 Comic-Con International: San Diego at San Diego Convention Center. Amy Sussman/Getty Images Ray Gunn director Brad Bird has made several lasting films in his day, but he has yet to venture into television. He hasn’t ruled out the opportunity to make a miniseries with a project he has long pursued. “Actually yeah, I have a project that, it’s hard to explain, but it would lend itself to multiple episodes,” he said at the “Brad Bird: My Cinematic Journey” panel at San Diego Comic-Con 2026, moderated by Collider’s Steven Weintraub in Hall H. “It has to do with the 1906 earthquake in San Francisco.” This answer was prompted by Patton Oswalt via video message, in which the Ratatouille voice actor asked if a multi-episode form project was in Bird’s future. Oswalt also snuck in a quick “and also Ratatouille 2 ?” Related Stories Casting Brad Bird’s ‘Ray Gunn’ Adds Sam Rockwell, Scarlett Johansson & Tom Waits To Voice Cast – First Look News Netflix Sets Release Date For Brad Bird’s ‘Ray Gunn’; Unveils Clip, Images For Sam Rockwell & Scarlett Johansson-Voiced Protagonists – Annecy Based on James Dalessandro’s 2004 historical novel about the catastrophic earthquake and fire that killed more than 3,000 people and destroyed more than 80% of San Francisco, Bird has sought to adapt the book since 2007. Watch on Deadline “There’s a lotta BS on the Internet about the reason it didn’t happen,” Bird added of the long-stewing project. “They said the budget went out of control or something like that. We never got that far.” Brad Bird teases that he has thought about “1906” as a TV series with eight episodes, prompted by Patron Oswalt, during his career retrospective panel at #SDCC in Hall H pic.twitter.com/HNXWtylc08 — Deadline (@DEADLINE) July 24, 2026 The Incredibles (2004) helmer likened the process to packing up supplies for a hot air balloon, and having to lighten the load in order for lift-off, losing his darlings in the process. “The problem with it is, the more you research that time, the more amazing it is,” Bird continued. “And the stories that are happening are insane. The deeper I dug, the more stuff I [had to include]. I was like, ‘This has to be in it!’ but it has to be big like a movie.”

title: Mary Kate Golding, ‘The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel’ Set Decorator, Dies at 34 in Stabbing
full_text: Skip to main content By McKinley Franklin Plus Icon McKinley Franklin View All July 23, 2026 6:46pm Mary Kate Golding Facebook Mary Kate Golding, The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel set decorator, has died after being found stabbed. She was 34. Golding was found dead on Tuesday in her apartment in Astoria, Queens, having suffered a stab wound on her back, an NYPD spokesperson told People . Police were called to her residence after midnight and at roughly 6:39 a.m. they responded to a call of “an unconscious person in the vicinity of Astoria Park South and Shore Boulevard.” That person was Golding’s husband, Joseph Azzaretto, with the police telling People they believe the deaths were a “possible murder-suicide.” Related Stories Lifestyle Anne Russell, Actress in 'Hello, Dolly!' and 'Mame,' Dies at 103 TV Zale Parry, Pioneering Scuba Diver and 'Sea Hunt' Actress, Dies at 93 Golding and Azzaretto’s 11-month old son was found at their residence unharmed. The Hollywood Reporter has reached out to the NYPD for comment. Golding is credited as the set decorator for the first six season-one episodes of The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel . According to her Facebook, she graduated from New York University in 2015. She has worked on a plethora of other television shows, including Gotham (2019); The Undoing (2020); The Flight Attendant (2020); season one of Harlem ; the 2021 Marvel series Hawkeye ; two 2024 episodes of Mr. and Mrs. Smith ; Goosebumps (2025); and the Netflix documentary Gone Girls: The Long Island Serial Killer . On the film side, she served as a member of the art department on the 2022 rom-com Marry Me , which starred Jennifer Lopez and Owen Wilson. She is also credited as an assistant set director on Sean Durkin’s forthcoming feature Deep Cuts , starring Drew Starkey and Cailee Spaeny. Years ago, Azzaretto’s younger brother, Nicholas Azzaretto, shot and killed his father at their Pennsylvania family home when he was 20. He pleaded guilty to involuntary manslaughter in November 2021 and was sentenced to 10 years of probation. THR

title: Cole Hauser On “Challenges” Of Making ‘Dutton Ranch’ Without Taylor Sheridan: “Everything Had Changed”
full_text: Skip to main content By Glenn Garner Glenn Garner Associate Editor @xoxo_glenn More Stories By Glenn Jon Bon Jovi Abruptly Ends MSG Show Amid Vocal Issues, Will Reschedule: “You’re Not Getting The Best Of Me” Cole Hauser On “Challenges” Of Making ‘Dutton Ranch’ Without Taylor Sheridan: “Everything Had Changed” John Leguizamo “Could Not See” For 12 To 14 Hours A Day Playing Blind ‘The Odyssey’ Role View All July 23, 2026 6:45pm Cole Hauser discusses making 'Dutton Ranch' without Taylor Sheridan. Rob Latour/PMC/Emerson Miller/Paramount+ Although the Duttons are still keeping things interesting on our screens, it’s a whole new behind-the-scenes family for the cast of Taylor Sheridan ‘s western TV franchise. Cole Hauser , who reprises his role as Rip Wheeler, recently opened up about the “challenges” of filming Dutton Ranch without parent series Yellowstone creator Taylor Sheridan at the helm, following Dutton Ranch creator, EP and director Chad Feehan’s departure after completing the first season. “There were challenges, you know?” explained Hauser to Film Inside NYC . “Not having Taylor [Sheridan] there, for us to really kind of be able to work with these new writers, these new directors, these people—everything had changed. Related Stories News Benjamin Cavell Joins ‘Dutton Ranch’ As Showrunner Following Chad Feehan Exit News Ed Harris Returning To ‘Dutton Ranch’ For Season 2 After Character Arc Frustrations & Showrunner Change Hauser noted the “only normality that we had whatsoever on set” was himself and returning Yellowstone co-stars Finn Little and Kelly Reilly, as well as director Christina Alexandra Voros. Watch on Deadline “I enjoy a challenge, it certainly was a challenge. We worked our asses off this year,” he added. “And I don’t think a lot of the outside noise or the thought of ‘what’s the weight of the show?’ was really apparent to me at the time.” View this post on Instagram While the new on-set environment was an adjustment for Hauser, he explained that the experience brought the cast closer together while filming the Paramount+ spin-off series. “It was about putting my head down and working my ass off with what I had around me. The writers that we had, the new writers that we had, that we had to figure out on our feet how to really perform and tell a story. So, I’m proud of what we accomplished, and I look forward to doing it again,” said Hauser. Last month, SEAL Team creator Benjamin Cavell was tapped to take over as showrunner for Season 2 of Dutton Ranch . As for why Feehan exited the series, sources tell Deadline he delivered as a writer, but faced challenges handling the bold-faced cast , a key component of a showrunner’s job.
